title: Magneto-Optical Detection of Anisotropic Spin Currents in Altermagnetic RuO2
authors: Joongwon Lee, Jeonglyul Kim, Sreejith Nair, Seung Gyo Jeong, Changi Kim, Jae-Pil So, Bohm-Jung Yang, Bharat Jalan, Hyobin Yoo, Farhan Rana, Taekoo Oh, Hong-Gyu Park
abstract: Altermagnets are a recently identified class of collinear antiferromagnets that host large spin-split electronic bands, offering a promising platform for efficient spin-current generation. Among proposed candidates, the metallic oxide RuO2 is predicted to exhibit strong altermagnetic spin splitting; however, whether it sustains robust magnetic order beyond the ultrathin thickness limit remains unresolved. Here, we employ optical probes to investigate charge-to-spin conversion in a 12-nm-thick (101)-oriented RuO2 film grown on sapphire. Polarization-resolved second-harmonic generation reveals nonlinear optical responses consistent with the surface symmetry and Néel order of RuO2. Under an applied current, both second-harmonic generation and polar magneto-optical Kerr effect measurements detect a pronounced, directionally anisotropic spin polarization, exhibiting enhanced signals for current along [010] and strongly suppressed responses for current along [-101], in agreement with the symmetry of the altermagnetic spin-splitter effect. Non-magnetic or Rashba-type mechanisms cannot explain this symmetry-selective response. Scanning transmission electron microscopy further reveals that substantial strain persists even in relatively thick films, providing a possible explanation for the observed behavior. Therefore, these results establish RuO2 as an efficient spin source and demonstrate the potential of altermagnets for field-free spintronic devices.
summary: This paper provides experimental evidence for the altermagnetic spin-splitter effect in RuO2 thin films using magneto-optical probes. By demonstrating directionally anisotropic spin accumulation, the study establishes RuO2 as a promising material for field-free spintronic devices.
main_problem: The study aims to determine if metallic RuO2 sustains robust altermagnetic order in films thicker than the ultrathin limit and to distinguish the intrinsic altermagnetic spin-splitter effect (ASSE) from extrinsic mechanisms like the Spin Hall Effect or Rashba-type splitting.
main_result: The researchers experimentally demonstrate directionally anisotropic spin accumulation in 12-nm RuO2 films, confirming the presence of the altermagnetic spin-splitter effect and establishing RuO2 as an efficient, symmetry-controlled spin source.
method: The study combines polarization-resolved Second-Harmonic Generation (SHG) and high-sensitivity polar Magneto-Optical Kerr Effect (MOKE) with 4D-STEM structural analysis and Kubo-formalism-based electronic structure calculations.
model_or_system: The physical system is a 12-nm-thick (1-01)-oriented RuO2 thin film grown on r-plane sapphire via molecular beam epitaxy, characterized by altermagnetic spin-split electronic bands and Néel order.
key_observables: SHG intensity and asymmetry, MOKE rotation (Kerr rotation), spin Hall angle, and local lattice strain.
important_parameters: 12-nm film thickness, Néel temperature (T_N ~ 500 K), current densities up to 6 x 10^6 A/cm^2, and MOKE sensitivity of ~10 nrad.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed optical signals are intrinsic to the altermagnetic spin accumulation and not artifacts of Joule heating or thermal gradients.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the crystallographic/magnetic structure, spin-split Fermi surfaces, and optical probing geometry; Figure 3 involves 4D-STEM analysis of structural clusters and strain mapping.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the altermagnetic properties of RuO2, details the experimental setup for optical and structural characterization, presents the symmetry-driven anisotropic spin response, discusses the role of strain and structural heterogeneity, and concludes with the implications for spintronics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
